passages.explanation: Herod the tetrarch hears the report about Jesus and tells his servants: this is John the Baptiser — he's risen from the dead. Matthew flashes back to explain: Herod had arrested John, bound him, and put him in prison because of Herodias, his brother Philip's wife. John had told Herod: it's not lawful for you to have her. Herod wanted to kill John but feared the multitude, who regarded him as a prophet. On Herod's birthday, Herodias' daughter dances before the guests. Herod is so pleased he swears to give her whatever she asks. Prompted by her mother, she asks for the head of John the Baptiser on a platter. The king is grieved but, because of his oaths and his guests, commands it. John is beheaded in prison. His head is brought on a platter and given to the girl, who takes it to her mother. John's disciples come, take the body, bury it, and go tell Jesus. A prophet dies because a king is too weak to break a foolish oath in front of dinner guests.

When Jesus hears about John, he withdraws by boat to a deserted place. The crowds hear of it and follow on foot from the cities. He comes out, sees the great multitude, is moved with compassion, and heals their sick. When evening comes, the disciples urge him to send the crowds away to buy food. Jesus says: they don't need to leave — you give them something to eat. They have only five loaves and two fish. Jesus takes them, looks up to heaven, blesses them, breaks them, and gives them to the disciples, who distribute them. Everyone eats and is satisfied. They pick up twelve baskets of leftovers. About five thousand men ate, besides women and children. The disciples see scarcity. Jesus sees an opportunity. The twelve baskets — one for each disciple — drive home the point: there's more than enough.

Immediately Jesus makes the disciples get into the boat and go ahead to the other side while he dismisses the crowds. He goes up on the mountain alone to pray. The boat is now far from land, beaten by waves, with the wind against them. In the fourth watch of the night — between 3 and 6 a.m. — Jesus comes to them walking on the sea. The disciples are terrified: it's a ghost! Jesus immediately speaks: cheer up! It's me. Don't be afraid. Peter answers: Lord, if it's you, command me to come to you on the water. Jesus says: come. Peter gets out of the boat, walks on the water toward Jesus, but when he sees the wind, he's afraid and begins to sink. He cries out: Lord, save me! Jesus immediately stretches out his hand, catches him, and says: you of little faith, why did you doubt? They get in the boat, and the wind ceases. Those in the boat worship him: truly you are the Son of God. Peter's failure isn't that he sank — it's that he looked away. But he's the only one who got out of the boat.

They cross over and come to the land of Gennesaret. The men of that place recognise Jesus and send word into all the surrounding region. They bring all who are sick and beg him to let them just touch the fringe of his garment. As many as touch it are made whole. Word about Jesus has outrun him. His reputation arrives before he does.
